html:
Our investigation has found a large gap between what The Renfrew Center promises and what it delivers.

By Amy Osborne and Mariana Best

May 5, 2026

DONE SHOT

attrs.bodyHtml: <iframe style="position: absolute; top: 0; left: 0; width: 100%; height: 100%; border: none;" src="https://media.journalism.berkeley.edu/video/player/?video=20260505_safia/playlist.m3u8&poster=20260505_safia/safia.jpg" width="1920" height="1080" frameborder="0" allowfullscreen scrolling="no"></iframe>
attrs.bodyText: <iframe style="position: absolute; top: 0; left: 0; width: 100%; height: 100%; border: none;" src="https://media.journalism.berkeley.edu/video/player/?video=20260505_safia/playlist.m3u8&poster=20260505_safia/safia.jpg" width="1920" height="1080" frameborder="0" allowfullscreen scrolling="no"></iframe>

html:
Eating disorders are among the deadliest of mental health conditions, yet they remain deeply misunderstood, difficult to treat, under-researched, and often lacking consensus around what “recovery” should even look like. As cases rise across the country, so too does the urgency to understand the systems designed to address them.

At the center of this story is the Renfrew Center, one of the most influential eating disorder treatment organizations in the United States. Since its founding in 1985, Renfrew has helped shape not only patient care, but also the broader standards and norms of the industry. In many ways, its model has become the blueprint others follow. But unlike many areas of medicine, eating disorder treatment operates in a space that is, in practice, largely self-regulated — which raises critical questions about oversight, accountability, and patient outcomes.

The Renfrew Center promotes its program as a highly individualized, research-backed approach – essentially that they have developed the “secret sauce” over decades of clinical experience and data collection for successful treatment. Its leadership has positioned the organization as a pioneer in the field, influencing how treatment is delivered far beyond its own centers. Renfrew even holds an annual conference for medical professionals and students and training sessions for their methods.

But there’s a problem.

In account after account, patients report leaving worse off than they went in.

[JOURNAL ENTRIES]

Our investigation found a significant gap between what Renfrew promises and what it delivers.

The organization advertises individualized treatment capable of addressing the full spectrum of eating disorders. In practice, however, we found a largely “one-size-fits-all” approach, where patients are placed into standardized, “cookie-cutter” protocols that often prioritize admissions and rigid metrics over individual needs.

[PULL QUOTE] “They don’t offer individualized care” – former employee who spoke on the condition of anonymity

attrs.caption: <em>All I really experienced there was people telling me what I was doing wrong and it just adds a new level of shame

attrs.bodyHtml: <iframe style="position: absolute; top: 0; left: 0; width: 100%; height: 100%; border: none;" src="https://media.journalism.berkeley.edu/video/player/?video=20260505_safia/playlist.m3u8&poster=20260505_safia/safia.jpg" width="1920" height="1080" frameborder="0" allowfullscreen scrolling="no"></iframe>
attrs.bodyText: <iframe style="position: absolute; top: 0; left: 0; width: 100%; height: 100%; border: none;" src="https://media.journalism.berkeley.edu/video/player/?video=20260505_safia/playlist.m3u8&poster=20260505_safia/safia.jpg" width="1920" height="1080" frameborder="0" allowfullscreen scrolling="no"></iframe>

html:
We also found that Renfrew’s trademarked Unified Treatment Model, its so-called “secret sauce” to treat eating disorders that is widely promoted and has become an industry standard, is described by many as deeply punitive and at times causing additional harm and trauma rather than fostering recovery.

Renfrew also promises “Unparalleled Care” in a “warm and nurturing environment.” But in interview after interview, former patients described something very different: feeling shamed, dismissed, or unsupported during moments of acute distress—whether during panic attacks, when raising concerns about treatment, or while struggling through meals.

Taken together, these accounts reveal a troubling disconnect between the care that is promised and the care that is experienced.

attrs.caption: <em>I was so scared. I was having panic attacks non stop. I felt like everything I did was being watched, and I had to unlearn everything that I learned at Renfrew to then start my recovery journey again

attrs.bodyHtml: <iframe style="position: absolute; top: 0; left: 0; width: 100%; height: 100%; border: none;" src="https://media.journalism.berkeley.edu/video/player/?video=20260505_safia/playlist.m3u8&poster=20260505_safia/safia.jpg" width="1920" height="1080" frameborder="0" allowfullscreen scrolling="no"></iframe>
attrs.bodyText: <iframe style="position: absolute; top: 0; left: 0; width: 100%; height: 100%; border: none;" src="https://media.journalism.berkeley.edu/video/player/?video=20260505_safia/playlist.m3u8&poster=20260505_safia/safia.jpg" width="1920" height="1080" frameborder="0" allowfullscreen scrolling="no"></iframe>

html:
We spoke with over a dozen interviews with former patients, several former employees, researchers, and advocates from across the country, each offering a different perspective on Renfrew’s system that is both widely trusted and increasingly questioned. We reviewed medical records, personal journals, institutional materials, and compared claims with available evidence to understand Renfrew’s treatment model and its effects on its patients.

What emerged is not a simple story of success or failure, but something more complicated: a for-profit system where standardized, protocol-driven care can override individual needs, even as some patients and clinical professionals report benefiting from its structure. At the same time, Renfrew’s influential treatment method is often treated as settled science despite limited evidence and eating disorder research that is under-studied and still evolving.

Close with coming back to Safia from Lead
